name: Word Counter
description: Check word count, character count, sentence count, paragraph count, and estimated reading time for any text.

faq.question: What can I check with the Word Counter?
faq.answer: You can check total words, characters, characters without spaces, sentences, paragraphs, reading time, speaking time, and average words per sentence.

faq.question: Does the Word Counter save my text?
faq.answer: No. The tool runs in your browser and is designed for quick private checks without an account.

faq.question: When should I use a word counter?
faq.answer: Use it when editing blog posts, essays, landing pages, emails, product descriptions, or social copy that needs a specific length.

name: Readability Checker
description: Review how easy your text is to read and improve clarity for blogs, academic writing, emails, and web content.

faq.question: What does the Readability Checker measure?
faq.answer: It reviews words, sentences, syllables, Flesch Reading Ease, Flesch-Kincaid Grade, and a simple reading level label.

faq.question: What readability score should I aim for?
faq.answer: For general web content, a clear and accessible score is usually better than a complex one. Match the reading level to your audience.

faq.question: Can readability affect SEO?
faq.answer: Clear writing can improve user experience, time on page, and conversions, which can support better content performance.

name: Keyword Density Checker
description: Use a free keyword density checker to review keyword frequency, spot repetition, and keep SEO content natural before publishing.

faq.question: What is keyword density?
faq.answer: Keyword density shows how often a word or phrase appears compared with the total word count of your content.

faq.question: Is there a perfect keyword density for SEO?
faq.answer: No single percentage guarantees rankings. Use density as a diagnostic to catch repetition and keep your writing natural.

faq.question: What does this checker help me find?
faq.answer: It helps identify repeated terms, top keywords, unique word count, and possible overuse in SEO-focused content.

guide.title: Check keyword use without writing for robots.
intro: This keyword density checker helps you see how often important words appear in your content. It is useful when you want to catch accidental repetition, compare keyword balance, or review an article before publishing.
Use it as a keyword density checker tool, keyword density analyzer, or SEO keyword density checker when you need a quick look at how naturally your target terms appear in a draft.
guide.stepsTitle: How to use the Keyword Density Checker

steps.title: Paste your draft
steps.text: Add your blog post, landing page copy, product description, or article draft into the text box.

steps.title: Review total and unique words
steps.text: Start with the overall word count and unique word count so you understand the size and variety of the text.

steps.title: Check repeated terms
steps.text: Look at the top words and phrases. If one term appears too often, read those sentences again and decide whether they sound natural.

steps.title: Compare with search intent
steps.text: A keyword density tool can show frequency, but your judgment decides whether the page actually answers the reader’s question.

steps.title: Edit for clarity first
steps.text: Use the results as a signal, not a target. Replace forced repetition with clear wording, examples, and related terms.

guide.exampleIntro: If a 1,000 word article uses the same exact keyword 35 times, the density is 3.5 percent. That number is not automatically bad, but it is a reason to reread the page and check whether the phrasing feels forced.

example.label: Next step
example.value: Read naturally and edit only forced repetition

bestPractices: Do not chase a perfect keyword density percentage.
Use your main keyword in important places, then write naturally through the rest of the page.
Mix exact terms with related language when it helps the reader.
Avoid repeating the same phrase in every paragraph.
Use a keyword density calculator as a review tool, not as a writing rule.
Focus on search intent, clarity, and complete answers before keyword frequency.
guide.relatedText: These guides explain keyword density, safe SEO ranges, checking workflows, keyword stuffing, and natural keyword use.

name: Meta Tag Generator
description: Create clean meta titles, descriptions, and essential head tags for pages that need better search presentation.

faq.question: What meta tags can this tool generate?
faq.answer: It can generate title, description, canonical, robots, Open Graph, and Twitter card tags for a web page.

faq.question: How long should a meta title be?
faq.answer: A practical target is usually around 50 to 60 characters so the title stays clear in search results.

faq.question: Why do meta descriptions matter?
faq.answer: Meta descriptions help set expectations in search results and can improve click-through rate when they are specific and useful.

name: CPC & ROAS Calculator
description: Estimate campaign efficiency, ad spend performance, and return on ad spend for practical decision-making.

faq.question: What is CPC?
faq.answer: CPC means cost per click. It shows how much you pay on average for each click from an ad campaign.

faq.question: What is ROAS?
faq.answer: ROAS means return on ad spend. It compares campaign revenue against ad spend to estimate efficiency.

faq.question: What numbers do I need for this calculator?
faq.answer: Enter ad spend, clicks, conversions, and revenue to calculate CPC, CPA, conversion rate, ROAS, and profit.

name: UTM Builder
description: Build UTM tracking links for paid campaigns, social posts, email promotions, and marketing experiments with a clean campaign URL builder.

faq.question: What is a UTM link?
faq.answer: A UTM link is a normal URL with tracking parameters added so analytics tools can identify campaign source, medium, and name.

faq.question: Which UTM fields are required?
faq.answer: The most important fields are source, medium, and campaign. Content and term are optional for more detailed tracking.

faq.question: When should I use the UTM Builder?
faq.answer: Use it before sharing links in email, ads, social posts, newsletters, or partnerships where campaign tracking matters.

guide.title: Create clean campaign tracking links without manual URL mistakes.
intro: This UTM Builder helps you create tracking URLs for Google Analytics, paid ads, email campaigns, social posts, partner links, and QR campaigns. Instead of typing UTM parameters by hand, you can enter your campaign details and generate a clean URL that is easier to measure later.
Use it as a UTM Generator, UTM Link Builder, Campaign URL Builder, or Tracking Link Generator whenever you need consistent UTM tracking across marketing channels.
guide.stepsTitle: How to use the UTM Builder

steps.title: Paste the destination URL
steps.text: Start with the final page you want visitors to reach, such as a landing page, product page, article, signup form, or offer page.

steps.title: Add campaign source
steps.text: Use the platform or sender as the source. Common examples include google, facebook, linkedin, newsletter, partner, or instagram.

steps.title: Add campaign medium
steps.text: Use the channel type as the medium. Common examples include cpc, email, social, referral, display, or qr.

steps.title: Name the campaign clearly
steps.text: Choose a campaign name that will still make sense later, such as summer_sale, ebook_launch, black_friday, or product_demo.

steps.title: Copy and test the tracking URL
steps.text: Copy the generated URL, open it in a new tab, and confirm it loads correctly before using it in ads, emails, posts, or QR codes.

guide.exampleIntro: For a newsletter campaign promoting a new guide, your tracking fields could look like this:
bestPractices: Use lowercase names so reports do not split the same source into multiple versions.
Use underscores or hyphens instead of spaces in UTM parameters.
Keep source, medium, and campaign naming consistent across every channel.
Do not add UTM parameters to internal links on your own website.
Document your naming rules if more than one person creates campaign URLs.
Use utm_content only when you need to compare buttons, creatives, placements, or link versions.
guide.relatedText: These guides explain the concepts behind campaign URLs, UTM parameters, Google Analytics tracking, and tracking URL examples.

name: QR Code Generator
description: Generate QR codes for links, landing pages, forms, promotions, product pages, and campaign access points.

faq.question: What can I put inside a QR code?
faq.answer: You can encode URLs, campaign links, messages, email addresses, phone numbers, forms, and other short text content.

faq.question: Which QR size should I choose?
faq.answer: Use a larger size for print or posters, and keep enough margin around the code so phones can scan it reliably.

faq.question: Can I download the QR code?
faq.answer: Yes. The tool supports downloading generated QR codes as PNG or SVG files.

name: QR / Barcode Scanner
description: Scan QR codes and supported barcodes through mobile or desktop camera workflows where HTTPS is available.

faq.question: Can this scanner use my camera?
faq.answer: Yes, supported browsers can scan QR codes through the camera when the site is opened over HTTPS.

faq.question: Can I scan an uploaded image?
faq.answer: Yes. You can upload an image containing a QR code or supported barcode and the tool will try to decode it.

faq.question: Why might scanning fail?
faq.answer: Scanning can fail when the image is blurry, too small, poorly lit, cropped, or uses a barcode format the browser does not support.
